params.description: Vertical data table appears showing Favorite Fruits data: Dates: 60, Cherries: 45, Bananas: 30, Apples: 15.
Scale Preview System panel appears displaying scales of 2, 5, and 10 with checkmarks indicating all three scales accommodate the Favorite Fruits dataset.

beats.text: Here's data about students' favorite fruits. The most votes went to Dates with 60. Scale of 1 doesn't make sense here because it would take 60 lines on the axis. That's too much!
Your job: pick the right scale. Which scale will you use?

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Favorite Fruits data: Dates: 60, Cherries: 45, Bananas: 30, Apples: 15.
Scale Preview System panel showing scales of 2, 5, and 10 with checkmarks.

beats.text: Why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Choose all of the reasons.
Why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Choose all of the reasons.
options: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

and.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

validator.description: Student selected all four reasons, correct

beats.text: Exactly. All values are multiples of 5, so scales of 5 and 10 both work to read the graph clearly. For data going up to 60, Scale of 10 gives you a cleaner graph.

Scale Preview System panel showing scales of 2, 5, and 10 with checkmarks.
